summary: The provided context for dataset 'q1' shows ten problems (q1 through q10) each carrying exactly 100 issues, with no further differentiating metadata such as issue type, status breakdown, or activity timestamps. Because every problem has an identical issue count and the focus dataset 'q1' itself is not detailed beyond this aggregate, no problem can be distinguished as having more traction or as being closer to solvable based on the available data. Additional metadata is required to produce reliable, actionable prioritization.
problems: All ten problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 open issues, preventing any data-driven ranking of activity or traction.
No issue-level metadata (type, severity, status, last-updated) is available, so common patterns in open issues cannot be identified.
No proof-status detail is provided despite the 'Proof Status' heading, leaving solvability assessments unsupported.
The dataset context for the focus problem 'q1' is not described beyond the aggregate issue count.
highlights: Comprehensive coverage: every problem in the dataset has tracked issues, indicating consistent monitoring across all ten problems.
Uniform issue counts suggest a structured, standardized issue-tracking process is in place.
A clear list of all problems (q1-q10) is available as a foundation for deeper analysis once metadata is added.

suggested_todos.title: Enrich issue records with metadata (type, severity, status, last-updated) to enable traction and pattern analysis
Populate the Proof Status section with per-problem proof states to assess solvability
Define activity metrics (recent commits, comments, edits) to rank problems by traction
Cluster open issues by category to surface common patterns across q1-q10
Establish a baseline report template that highlights deltas week-over-week once richer data exists
